Tense

blocks.text_en: Tense
Formal Tense Grammar Explanation
What is Tense?
Why tense is important in Korean

blocks.text_ko: 한국어에서 서술어(동사·형용사)는 문장의 맨 끝에 옵니다. 서술어가 문장의 가장 중요한 내용을 결정합니다.
blocks.text_en: In Korean, the predicate (verb/adjective) comes at the very end of the sentence — and it determines the most important meaning.

blocks.text_ko: 서술어를 표현하는 다양한 문법이 있지만, 그중에서 가장 기본적이고 많이 쓰는 것이 동사·형용사의 현재 · 과거 · 미래입니다.
blocks.text_en: Many grammar patterns express predicates, but the most basic and most frequently used are the present, past, and future of verbs/adjectives.

blocks.text_ko: 동사·형용사의 현재·과거·미래는 한국어 공부의 기초 중의 기초입니다. 잘 이해하고, 충분히 연습하세요.
blocks.text_en: These three tenses are the most fundamental basics of Korean. Understand them well and practice diligently.
I eat rice.
I ate rice.
I will eat rice.

blocks.text_ko: 시제는 동사와 형용사의 시간을 표현하는 문법입니다. 한국어 시제에는 세 가지가 있습니다.
blocks.text_en: Tense is the grammar that expresses time in verbs and adjectives. Korean has three tenses.
Present
Things happening now
I eat.
Past
Things already done
I ate.
Future
Things to do ahead
I will eat.
Three Speech Styles in Korean

blocks.text_ko: 한국어에는 같은 뜻이라도 상황에 따라 다른 문체로 말하고 씁니다.
blocks.text_en: In Korean, you use different speech styles depending on the situation, even for the same meaning.
Formal
Spoken
Written
Form
Example
Tone
Where Used
-ㅂ니다 / 습니다-ㅂ니까 / 습니까?
-아요 / 어요 (polite)-아 / 어 (casual)
-ㄴ다 / 는다-았다 / 었다
먹습니다 / 먹습니까?
먹어요 / 먹어
먹는다 / 먹었다
official, polite
soft, friendly
literary, objective

blocks.text_ko: 뉴스, 발표, 회의, 연장자·처음 만난 사람과의 대화
blocks.text_en: news, presentations, meetings, elders, first meetings
daily conversation, friends & family

blocks.text_ko: 신문, 책, 보고서, 논문, TOPIK II 쓰기
blocks.text_en: news articles, books, reports, TOPIK II writing
In this textbook, we will learn the formal style (-ㅂ니다/습니다) first.

blocks.text_ko: 격식체는 규칙이 가장 간단하고, 어떤 상황에서든 예의 바르게 사용할 수 있기 때문입니다.
blocks.text_en: Because the formal style has the simplest rules and can be used politely in any situation.
What is the Base Form of a Predicate?

blocks.text_ko: 한국어 사전에서 단어를 찾으면 동사와 형용사는 항상 '~다'로 끝나는 형태로 나옵니다.
blocks.text_en: When you look up a word in a Korean dictionary, verbs and adjectives always appear in a form ending with '-다'.
Verbs · Action
Adjectives · Description
가다
먹다
마시다
공부하다
읽다
크다
작다
예쁘다
좋다
맛있다

blocks.text_ko: 이 형태를 '기본형'이라고 부릅니다. 한국어 사전에서 단어를 찾을 때 보이는 표준형입니다. 모든 활용형의 출발점이 되는 모양입니다.
blocks.text_en: This shape is called the 기본형 (base form). It is the standard form found when looking up a word in a Korean dictionary. It is the starting point from which every conjugated form is derived.
Base Form = Stem + 다 (Ending)
Base Form Formula

blocks.text_ko: 어간(語幹)은 단어의 줄기(stem)로 의미를 담고 있는 부분입니다.
blocks.text_en: The stem (어간) carries the meaning of the word.
The Role of the Ending '-다'

blocks.text_ko: 한국어 동사·형용사의 기본형은 '-다' 로 끝납니다. '-다' 는 사전에 등재될 때 쓰는 기본형 어미로, 활용 시 다른 어미로 교체되어 시제·문체를 표현합니다.
blocks.text_en: Every Korean verb and adjective base form ends in '-다'. The ending '-다' is the dictionary-citation ending; during conjugation it is replaced by other endings to express tense and speech style.
가다
먹다
공부하다

blocks.text_ko: -다로 끝나는 말은 그 문장의 서술어임을 나타냅니다.
blocks.text_en: A word ending in '-다' signals that it is the predicate of the sentence — the part that completes the meaning.

blocks.text_ko: 한국어는 형용사도 동사처럼 활용합니다. 형용사도 시제와 문체에 따라 형태가 변합니다.
blocks.text_en: In Korean, adjectives conjugate just like verbs. Adjectives also change form to express tense and speech style.

blocks.text_ko: 단어의 형태가 변하는 것을 활용 (活用)이라고 합니다.
blocks.text_en: When a word changes its form to fit the sentence, that change is called "conjugation" (활용).

blocks.text_ko: 하나의 기본형이, 시제(현재·과거·미래)나 말투(격식체·구어체 등)에 맞춰 여러 모양으로 옷을 갈아입는 것입니다.
blocks.text_en: One base form "changes clothes" into many shapes — depending on tense (present / past / future) and style (formal / casual). The meaning stays, only the ending changes.

blocks.text_ko: 앞부분(어간)은 그대로! 뒷부분(어미)만 바뀝니다. 그래서 한 단어로 여러 의미를 표현할 수 있습니다.
blocks.text_en: The front part (stem) stays the same — only the ending (어미) changes. That's how one word expresses many meanings.

blocks.text_ko: 이제 기본형을 현재 · 과거 · 미래형 격식체로 바꾸는 방법을 배워 봅시다.
blocks.text_en: Now let's learn how to change the base form into the present, past, and future formal forms.
How to Make Present
Remove '-다'
Remove '-다' and you get the stem.
Check the last letter of the stem

blocks.text_ko: 한국어 글자는 자음(consonant)과 모음(vowel)의 조합으로 만들어집니다.
blocks.text_en: Korean letters are made by combining consonants and vowels.
The consonant at the bottom of a letter is called '받침' (final consonant).
Add the ending by rule
🔊 Pronunciation note: '~ㅂ니다' / '~습니다'

blocks.text_ko: 받침 ㅂ과 ㄴ(부드러운 발음)이 만나면 ㅂ이 부드러운 발음인 ㅁ으로 바뀝니다. 그래서 읽을 때는 [ㅁ니다], [씀니다]로 읽어야 합니다.
blocks.text_en: When the final consonant ㅂ meets ㄴ (a softer sound), the ㅂ changes into the softer sound ㅁ. So when reading, you should pronounce it as 'ㅁ니다' or '씀니다'.
Check the last letter of the 어간 (stem)!
How to Make Past
Remove '-다' (same as present tense)
Remove '-다' and you get the stem.
Check the last vowel of the stem

blocks.text_ko: 한국어 모음은 크게 두 그룹으로 나뉩니다.
blocks.text_en: Korean vowels are divided into two groups.
Add the ending by rule

blocks.text_ko: 어간 끝이 자음이 아니라 모음일 때 '았/었'과 합쳐져서 짧아집니다. 이것을 '줄임(contraction)'이라고 합니다.
blocks.text_en: When the stem ends with a vowel, it merges with '았/었' and becomes shorter. This is called "contraction".
Words ending in '하다' always become 했습니다 / 했습니까.

blocks.text_ko: 어간의 마지막 모음이 'ㅡ'이면 'ㅡ'가 사라지고 그 앞 음절의 모음을 확인합니다.
blocks.text_en: When the last vowel of the stem is 'ㅡ', the 'ㅡ' disappears and you check the vowel of the syllable before it.

blocks.text_ko: 받침 'ㄷ'으로 끝나는 일부 동사는 모음 앞에서 'ㄷ'이 'ㄹ'로 바뀝니다.
blocks.text_en: In some verbs ending with 받침 'ㄷ', the 'ㄷ' changes to 'ㄹ' before a vowel.

blocks.text_ko: ⚠️ 주의: 모든 'ㄷ' 받침 동사가 불규칙은 아닙니다.
blocks.text_en: Not all ㄷ-ending verbs are irregular — these are regular; 'ㄷ' stays as-is and takes 았/었.

blocks.text_ko: 아래 동사들은 규칙형입니다. 'ㄷ'이 변하지 않고 그대로 '았/었습니다'가 붙습니다.
blocks.text_en: In some words ending with 받침 'ㅂ', the 'ㅂ' changes to '우' before a vowel.

blocks.text_ko: 받침 'ㅂ'으로 끝나는 일부 형용사와 동사는 모음 앞에서 'ㅂ'이 '우'로 바뀝니다.
blocks.text_en: Not all ㅂ-ending words are irregular — these are regular; 'ㅂ' stays as-is and takes 았/었.

blocks.text_ko: ⚠️ 주의: 모든 'ㅂ' 받침 단어가 불규칙은 아닙니다.
blocks.text_en: When the stem ends with '르', it changes and an extra 'ㄹ' is added to the previous syllable.

blocks.text_ko: 아래 동사들은 규칙형입니다. 'ㅂ'이 바뀌지 않고 그대로 '았/었습니다'가 붙습니다.
blocks.text_en: In some verbs ending with 'ㅅ', the 'ㅅ' disappears before a vowel.

blocks.text_ko: 어간이 '르'로 끝나면 '르'가 'ㄹ'로 바뀌고 앞 음절에 'ㄹ' 받침이 추가됩니다.
blocks.text_en: Check the last vowel, 받침, or shape of the stem and apply the matching rule.
How to Make Future
Key Structure of Future Tense

blocks.text_ko: 좋은 소식: 미래형은 과거형보다 훨씬 쉽습니다!
blocks.text_en: Good news: The future tense is much easier than the past tense!

blocks.text_ko: 미래형에서는 다시 받침(final consonant)만 확인하면 됩니다. 현재형과 같은 방식입니다!
blocks.text_en: In the future tense, you only need to check the 받침 again. It works the same way as the present tense!
어간(stem) + ㄹ / 을 + 것입니다 / 것입니까
Future Tense Irregulars

blocks.text_ko: 미래형 어미 '~을 것입니다'는 모음(ㅡ)으로 시작하므로, 받침이 모음 앞에서 변하는 일부 불규칙이 다시 나타납니다. 대상은 ㄷ · ㅂ · ㅅ 세 가지입니다.
blocks.text_en: The future ending '~을 것입니다' starts with a vowel (ㅡ), so the irregulars that change before vowels reappear: ㄷ, ㅂ, ㅅ.

blocks.text_ko: 참고: 'ㅡ 불규칙'과 '르 불규칙'은 어간이 모음으로 끝나기 때문에 미래형에서는 그냥 'ㄹ 것입니다'를 붙이는 규칙형이 됩니다. (예: 쓰다 → 쓸 것입니다, 다르다 → 다를 것입니다)
blocks.text_en: Note: 'ㅡ' and '르' irregulars have vowel-ending stems, so in the future tense they simply add 'ㄹ 것입니다' (regular). (e.g., 쓰다 → 쓸 것입니다, 다르다 → 다를 것입니다)
Summary
Check the last letter of the 어간 (stem)!
Summary

blocks.text_ko: 어간(stem)의 마지막 모음·받침·형태를 확인하고 아래 규칙을 적용하세요.
blocks.text_en: Check the last vowel, 받침, or shape of the stem and apply the matching rule.
Summary
Check the last letter of the 어간 (stem)!
No 받침 — add 'ㅂ' under the last letter, then 니다 (statement) or 니까 (question).
Has 받침 — just add 습니다 (statement) or 습니까 (question) right after the stem.
If 받침 is ㄹ — drop the ㄹ first, then follow Rule 🅐.

blocks.text_ko: 🅐 마지막 모음이 ㅏ / ㅗ → 았습니다 / 았습니까
blocks.text_en: 🅐 Last vowel ㅏ / ㅗ → 았습니다 / 았습니까

blocks.text_ko: 🅑 마지막 모음이 ㅏ / ㅗ가 아니면 → 었습니다 / 었습니까
blocks.text_en: 🅑 Last vowel not ㅏ / ㅗ → 었습니다 / 었습니까
contraction
🅓 ~하다 → 했습니다 / 했습니까
ㅡ Irregular
ㄷ Irregular
ㅂ Irregular
르 Irregular
ㅅ Irregular

blocks.text_ko: 🅰 받침이 없으면 → ㄹ 것입니다 / ㄹ 것입니까
blocks.text_en: 🅐 No 받침 → ㄹ 것입니다 / ㄹ 것입니까

blocks.text_ko: 🅱 받침이 있으면 → 을 것입니다 / 을 것입니까
blocks.text_en: 🅑 Has 받침 → 을 것입니다 / 을 것입니까
🅒 받침 = ㄹ → keep stem + 것입니다
ㄷ Irregular
ㅂ Irregular
ㅅ Irregular
Tense Grammar (Formal Style)

units.title_en: Learning Korean Particles

blocks.text_en: Learning Korean Particles
What is a Particle?
In English, the meaning is determined by the order of words.

blocks.text_ko: "I"가 동사(eat) 앞에 있으니까 주어(subject)이고 "rice"가 동사(eat) 뒤에 있으니까 목적어(object)입니다.
blocks.text_en: "I" comes before the verb (eat) so it's the subject. "Rice" comes after the verb (eat) so it's the object.

blocks.text_ko: 한국어에서는 단어 순서 대신 단어 뒤에 붙는 작은 글자가 역할을 알려 줍니다.
blocks.text_en: In Korean, instead of word order, small letters attached after a word tell you its role.

items.ko: 저 + 는 → "저"가 주어(subject)라는 표시 (marks "저" as the subject)
밥 + 을 → "밥"이 목적어(object)라는 표시 (marks "밥" as the object)

blocks.text_ko: 이 작은 글자를 "조사"라고 합니다. 영어로는 "particle"이라고 합니다.
blocks.text_en: These small letters are called "조사" (particles).

blocks.text_ko: 조사는 단어 뒤에 붙어서 "이 단어가 문장에서 무슨 역할을 하는지" 알려 줍니다.
blocks.text_en: Particles attach after a word and tell you "what role this word plays in the sentence."

blocks.text_ko: 중요한 점: 많은 조사는 앞 단어의 받침에 따라 모양이 달라집니다.
blocks.text_en: Important: Many particles change their form depending on the 받침 of the word before them.

blocks.text_ko: 하지만 모양이 달라지지 않는 조사도 있습니다. (예: 에, 에서, 에게)
blocks.text_en: However, some particles always stay the same. (e.g., 에, 에서, 에게)

blocks.text_ko: 이것은 각 조사를 배울 때 하나씩 알려 드리겠습니다.
blocks.text_en: We will explain this one by one as you learn each particle.

blocks.text_ko: 시제(tense)를 배울 때 받침을 배웠습니다. 그 규칙이 여기서도 쓰입니다!
blocks.text_en: You learned about 받침 when studying tenses. That same rule is used here too!

items.ko: 받침 있음 (받침 O): 빵, 선생님, 사랑
받침 없음 (받침 X): 저, 커피, 사과

blocks.text_en: Subject Markers
Let's look at an English sentence.
The word that does the action, like "I" here, is called "주어" (subject).
It's the same in Korean. Let's look at examples.

items.ko: 선생님은 학교에 갑니다. (The teacher goes to school.)

blocks.text_en: Like this, the answer to "who?" or "what?" is the subject.

blocks.text_ko: 한국어에서는 주어 뒤에 "은/는" 또는 "이/가"를 붙여서 "이것이 주어입니다"라고 알려 줍니다.
blocks.text_en: In Korean, you attach "은/는" or "이/가" after the subject to say "this is the subject."

blocks.text_ko: 둘 다 같은 뜻입니다. 둘 다 주어를 표시합니다.
blocks.text_en: Both mean the same thing. Both mark the subject.
Which one should you use?
In general, "은/는" is used more often.

blocks.text_ko: 주어를 더 강조하고 싶을 때 "이/가"를 사용합니다.
blocks.text_en: "이/가" is used when you want to emphasize the subject more.

items.ko: 저는 학생입니다. (I am a student.) — 일반적인 표현 (general)
제가 학생입니다. (I am the student.) — 강조 (emphasis)

blocks.text_ko: 하지만 처음 한국어를 배울 때 이 차이는 걱정하지 않아도 됩니다!
blocks.text_en: But when you first learn Korean, you don't need to worry about this difference!

blocks.text_ko: 어떤 것을 써도 둘 다 주어입니다. 둘 다 맞습니다.
blocks.text_en: Whichever you use, both are the subject. Both are correct.

blocks.text_ko: 이 미묘한 차이는 한국어를 배워가면서 자연스럽게 알게 될 것입니다.
blocks.text_en: This subtle difference will become clear naturally as you learn more Korean.

blocks.text_ko: 지금은 이것만 기억하세요: 은/는과 이/가는 모두 주어를 표시합니다!
blocks.text_en: For now, just remember this: Both 은/는 and 이/가 mark the subject!

blocks.text_ko: 규칙은 간단합니다. 앞 단어의 받침에 따라 모양이 달라집니다.
blocks.text_en: The rule is simple. The form changes depending on the 받침 of the word before it.

blocks.text_ko: 왜 두 가지가 있을까요? 발음하기 쉽게 하기 위해서입니다.
blocks.text_en: Why are there two forms? To make pronunciation easier.
"빵는" is hard to say. "빵은" is easy!

blocks.raw: 받침 있음 (받침 O)받침 없음 (받침 X) 일반 (general)은는 강조 (emphasis)이가

blocks.text_en: 은/는 예:

blocks.raw: 단어받침→ 은/는예문 저 (I)없음 (no)저는저는 학생입니다. (I am a student.) 커피 (coffee)없음 (no)커피는커피는 맛있습니다. (Coffee is delicious.) 사과 (apple)없음 (no)사과는사과는 빨갑니다. (The apple is red.) 날씨 (weather)없음 (no)날씨는날씨는 좋습니다. (The weather is good.) 빵 (bread)있음 (yes)빵은빵은 맛있습니다. (Bread is delicious.) 선생님 (teacher)있음 (yes)선생님은선생님은 친절합니다. (The teacher is kind.) 사랑 (love)있음 (yes)사랑은사랑은 아름답습니다. (Love is beautiful.) 행복 (happiness)있음 (yes)행복은행복은 중요합니다. (Happiness is important.)

blocks.text_en: 이/가 예:

blocks.raw: 단어받침→ 이/가예문 저 (I)없음 (no)제가*제가 갑니다. (I will go.) 커피 (coffee)없음 (no)커피가커피가 있습니다. (There is coffee.) 사과 (apple)없음 (no)사과가사과가 맛있습니다. (The apple is delicious.) 날씨 (weather)없음 (no)날씨가날씨가 좋습니다. (The weather is good.) 빵 (bread)있음 (yes)빵이빵이 있습니다. (There is bread.) 선생님 (teacher)있음 (yes)선생님이선생님이 옵니다. (The teacher is coming.) 사랑 (love)있음 (yes)사랑이사랑이 필요합니다. (Love is needed.) 꽃 (flower)있음 (yes)꽃이꽃이 예쁩니다. (The flower is pretty.)

blocks.text_en: *Special rule: "저 + 가" = "제가" ("저" changes to "제" for natural pronunciation.)
Object Marker
First, let's learn the word "목적어."
Let's look at an English sentence.

blocks.text_ko: 여기서 "rice"처럼 행동의 대상이 되는 단어를 "목적어(object)"라고 합니다.
blocks.text_en: The word that receives the action, like "rice" here, is called "목적어" (object).
Simply put, the answer to "what?" is the object.

blocks.text_ko: "을/를"은 이 단어가 동작을 받는 목적어인 것을 알려 주는 조사입니다.
blocks.text_en: "을/를" is a particle that tells you this word is the object receiving the action.

blocks.text_ko: 한국어에서는 목적어 뒤에 "을" 또는 "를"을 붙여서 "이것이 목적어입니다"라고 알려 줍니다.
blocks.text_en: In Korean, you attach "을" or "를" after the object to say "this is the object."

blocks.raw: 단어받침→ 을/를예문 커피 (coffee)없음 (no)커피를커피를 마십니다. (I drink coffee.) 사과 (apple)없음 (no)사과를사과를 먹습니다. (I eat an apple.) 한국어 (Korean)없음 (no)한국어를한국어를 공부합니다. (I study Korean.) 빵 (bread)있음 (yes)빵을빵을 삽니다. (I buy bread.) 꽃 (flower)있음 (yes)꽃을꽃을 좋아합니다. (I like flowers.) 사랑 (love)있음 (yes)사랑을사랑을 합니다. (I love.)

blocks.text_en: Summary So Far: Making a Sentence

blocks.raw: [누가] 은/는 + [무엇을] 을/를 + 동사 WHO WHAT VERB

blocks.text_en: And (connecting words)

blocks.text_ko: "와/과"는 두 개의 단어를 연결할 때 사용합니다. 영어의 "and"와 같습니다.
blocks.text_en: "와/과" is used to connect two words. It is the same as "and" in English.

blocks.raw: 단어받침→ 와/과예문 저 (I)없음 (no)저와저와 같이 갑시다. (Let's go together with me.) 커피 (coffee)없음 (no)커피와커피와 빵을 삽니다. (I buy coffee and bread.) 사과 (apple)없음 (no)사과와사과와 빵을 먹습니다. (I eat apple and bread.) 빵 (bread)있음 (yes)빵과빵과 커피를 삽니다. (I buy bread and coffee.) 선생님 (teacher)있음 (yes)선생님과선생님과 학생이 있습니다. (There is a teacher and student.) 사랑 (love)있음 (yes)사랑과사랑과 행복은 중요합니다. (Love and happiness are important.)

blocks.text_en: Or (choosing between options)

blocks.text_ko: "나/이나"는 여러 개 중에서 하나를 고를 때 사용합니다. 영어의 "or"와 같습니다.
blocks.text_en: "나/이나" is used when choosing one from several options. It is the same as "or" in English.

blocks.raw: 단어받침→ 나/이나예문 저 (I)없음 (no)저나저나 당신이 갑니다. (I or you will go.) 커피 (coffee)없음 (no)커피나커피나 사과를 삽니다. (I buy coffee or apple.) 사과 (apple)없음 (no)사과나사과나 빵을 먹습니다. (I eat apple or bread.) 빵 (bread)있음 (yes)빵이나빵이나 사과를 먹습니다. (I eat bread or apple.) 영화 (movie)없음 (no)영화나영화나 여행을 좋아합니다. (I like movie or travel.) 행복 (happiness)있음 (yes)행복이나행복이나 사랑을 원합니다. (I want happiness or love.)

blocks.text_en: All Rules at a Glance

blocks.raw: 역할받침 있음 (받침 O)받침 없음 (받침 X)영어 주어 - 일반 (Subject - general)은는subject (general) 주어 - 강조 (Subject - emphasis)이가subject (emphasis) 목적어 (Object)을를object marker 그리고 (And)과와and 또는 (Or)이나나or

blocks.text_en: To (giving or telling someone)

blocks.text_ko: "에게"는 사람이나 동물에게 무엇을 주거나 말할 때 사용합니다.
blocks.text_en: "에게" is used when giving something or telling something to a person or animal.
It is the same as "to" in English.

items.en: I give a book to Sumi.

blocks.text_en: Important difference: "에게" vs "에"

blocks.raw: 단어사람/동물?→ 에게/에예문 저 (I)사람저에게 / 제게저에게 주세요. (Please give it to me.) 선생님 (teacher)사람선생님에게선생님에게 말합니다. (I tell the teacher.) 학생 (student)사람학생에게학생에게 가르칩니다. (I teach the student.) 수미 (Sumi)사람수미에게수미에게 전화합니다. (I call Sumi.) 고양이 (cat)동물고양이에게고양이에게 밥을 줍니다. (I give food to the cat.) 나무 (tree)식물나무에나무에 물을 줍니다. (I give water to the tree.) 꽃 (flower)식물꽃에꽃에 물을 줍니다. (I give water to the flower.)

blocks.text_en: "에게" always has the same form regardless of 받침!
At / In / On (Time)
"에" is attached before time words to tell you "when?"
It is the same as "at / in / on" in English.

blocks.raw: 시간 단어+ 에예문 아침 (morning)아침에아침에 운동합니다. (I exercise in the morning.) 점심 (lunch)점심에점심에 밥을 먹습니다. (I eat at lunch.) 저녁 (dinner)저녁에저녁에 공부합니다. (I study in the evening.) 밤 (night)밤에밤에 잠을 잡니다. (I sleep at night.) 한 시 (1 o'clock)한 시에한 시에 만납니다. (I meet at 1 o'clock.) 일요일 (Sunday)일요일에일요일에 쉽니다. (I rest on Sunday.) 주말 (weekend)주말에주말에 여행합니다. (I travel on the weekend.) 내년 (next year)내년에내년에 한국에 갑니다. (I go to Korea next year.)
<caution>

blocks.text_ko: 주의! "에"를 붙이지 않는 시간 단어가 있습니다!
blocks.text_en: Caution! Some time words do NOT use "에"!

items.ko: 이번 주 (this week) → 이번 주 바쁩니다. (NOT 이번 주에)

blocks.text_ko: 이 단어들은 이미 "지금과 가까운 시간"을 나타내기 때문에 "에"가 필요 없습니다.
blocks.text_en: These words already indicate "a time close to now" so they don't need "에."
At / In (Place of Action)
"에서" is attached to a place word to tell you "where?"
It means "doing an action at/in a certain place."
It is the same as "at / in" in English.

blocks.raw: 장소 단어+ 에서예문 학교 (school)학교에서학교에서 공부합니다. (I study at school.) 대학교 (university)대학교에서대학교에서 한국어를 배웁니다. (I learn Korean at university.) 교실 (classroom)교실에서교실에서 수업합니다. (I have class in the classroom.) 도서관 (library)도서관에서도서관에서 책을 읽습니다. (I read books in the library.) 집 (house)집에서집에서 쉽니다. (I rest at home.) 방 (room)방에서방에서 잠을 잡니다. (I sleep in the room.) 커피숍 (coffee shop)커피숍에서커피숍에서 커피를 마십니다. (I drink coffee at the coffee shop.) 공원 (park)공원에서공원에서 운동합니다. (I exercise in the park.) 시장 (market)시장에서시장에서 과일을 삽니다. (I buy fruit at the market.) 공항 (airport)공항에서공항에서 기다립니다. (I wait at the airport.)

blocks.text_en: "에서" also always has the same form regardless of 받침!
Complete Summary

blocks.raw: 받침에 따라 모양이 바뀌는 조사: Particles that change form based on 받침: 받침 있음 받침 없음 (받침 O) (받침 X) 주어(일반): 은 는 (subject - general) 주어(강조): 이 가 (subject - emphasis) 목적어: 을 를 (object) 그리고: 과 와 (and) 또는: 이나 나 (or) 항상 같은 모양인 조사: Particles that always stay the same: ~에게 사람/동물에게 줄 때 (to someone) ~에 시간을 말할 때 (at/in/on + time) ~에서 장소에서 행동할 때 (at/in + place)

blocks.text_en: Sentence Building Practice

blocks.raw: [누가] 은/는 [언제] 에 [어디에서] 에서 [무엇을] 을/를 동사 WHO WHEN WHERE WHAT VERB

items.en: I study Korean at school in the morning.
The teacher reads a book at home in the evening.

items.ko: 수미는 주말에 커피숍에서 커피와 빵을 먹습니다.
items.en: Sumi eats coffee and bread at the coffee shop on the weekend.

items.ko: 저는 내년에 한국에서 한국어를 배울 것입니다.
items.en: I will learn Korean in Korea next year.

translations.text: Universal can / can't — covers both learned skills and situational possibility.
For learned skills only, the two are fully interchangeable. Possibility cannot use 줄 알다.
① 능력과 가능 — '~을/ㄹ 수 있다 / 없다' handles both senses of can.
Formation — same 받침 rule as other ㄹ/을 patterns:
② 능력일 때 동의어 — '~을/ㄹ 수 있다 / 없다' = '~을/ㄹ 줄 알다 / 모르다' for learned skills only.
Formation — same 받침 rule for 줄 알다:
Side-by-side examples — when synonyms work, when only 수 있다 works:
Korean has two ways to make suggestions — one is a question (Shall we...?), the other a statement (Let's...!).
How to make '~ㄹ/을까요?' — same 받침 rule as other ㄹ/을 patterns.
How to make '~ㅂ시다/읍시다' — same 받침 rule.
Used in pairs & no subject needed. Both '~ㄹ까요?' and '~ㅂ시다' already imply we — no need to say 우리.
The most basic Korean sentence is Subject + Predicate. In Structure 2, the predicate is a verb, describing what someone does (or did).
All 10 sentences below are in past tense, using the same subject He = 그는. Apply one of the four rules below to change the verb.
Korean has two ways to negate verbs/adjectives: a short form and a long form. Both mean the same thing.
안 vs 못 — different meanings! Choose carefully.
~하다 verbs/adjectives prefer the LONG form. Short-form 안 sounds unnatural before ~하다.
이다's negation is a different word — 아니다. Also, the subject particle changes!
Negating commands and suggestions uses the verb 말다 (to stop / not do) — NOT 안 or 못.
To say I want to [verb] in Korean, attach '~고 싶다' to the verb stem.
'~고 싶다' has NO irregular forms — same easy rule as '~고 있다' (Grammar 2). Just stem + 고 + 싶다. Always!
Three stem + 고 patterns to compare. Don't confuse them!

The form of ~고 싶다 changes by the subject's person. 1st person (I/we) uses ~고 싶다 for one's own wishes. 2nd person (you) takes the question form ~고 싶습니까? to ask about another's wishes. 3rd person (he/she/they) uses ~고 싶어 하다 to describe someone else's wishes.
Grammar 6 vs Grammar 7 — '~고 싶다' is for what YOU want to DO. '~았/었으면 좋겠다' is for wishing a SITUATION were true (often beyond your direct control).
How to make '~았/었으면 좋겠다' — start from the past tense stem, then add 으면 좋겠습니다.
Past tense FORM, but NOT a past meaning! The 았/었 here marks a wish about the present or future — exactly like English I wish I HAD a car.
The subject (저는) is usually omitted. In wish sentences, the speaker is understood from context.
To express a plan or intention (be going to), attach '~려고 하다' to the verb stem.
Meaning of '~려고 하다': a person's plan or intention for a future action.
'~려고 하다' is for the NEAR future — same meaning as English be about to.

'~려고 하다' (plan) vs '~려고' (purpose) — same start, different roles. ~려고 하다 is a complete predicate (the main verb of the sentence). ~려고 is a connector meaning in order to, linked to a main verb that follows.
To express a decision, resolution, or promise, attach '~기로 했다' to the verb stem.
Always past tense (했습니다)! The verb 하다 stays in the past form because the decision was already made in the past, even if the action will happen in the future.
'~기로 했다' is a SHORTENED form. The words 결정 (decision), 결심 (resolution), or 약속 (promise) are omitted but understood from context.
Korean speakers naturally understand which sense (결정 / 결심 / 약속) is implied from context.
Compare intention expressions (Grammar 6, 8, 9). They differ in how committed the speaker is.
Four related expressions — permission, prohibition, obligation, exemption. They are not used as a set, but learning them together helps you grasp the contrasts.
Plus from Grammar 5: ~지 마세요 = Don't! (negative command — direct, person-to-person).
Three patterns share the SAME 아/어 vowel rule — permission, obligation, and exemption all use 아/어 (same rule as past tense).
✅ 허락 / Permission — stem + 아/어 + 도 됩니다
❗ 의무 / Obligation — stem + 아/어 + 야 됩니다 / 야 합니다
FREE 면제 / Exemption — 안 + stem + 아/어 + 도 됩니다
Prohibition (~(으)면 안 됩니다) uses a different rule — the 받침 rule (NOT the vowel rule).
5 patterns × 3 verbs reference table — see how each verb conjugates across all five patterns.
Two key insights to avoid confusion:
① Must not vs Don't! — different nuance:
② May and Don't have to share the same base — just add 안:
Korean has two levels of polite request — both attach to the verb stem.
How to make ~아/어 주세요 — vowel rule (last stem vowel decides 아/어).
ㅂ-irregular verbs: 돕다 (help) → 도와. The ㅂ becomes 오/우 before a vowel ending.
Word order: Korean requests typically omit the subject (you = listener). Adverbs like 다시 (again), 잠깐만 (a moment) come BEFORE the verb.
Korean has two ways to talk about experience — formal have/never and casual try doing.
How to make ~ㄴ/은 적이 있다 — 받침 rule (no final consonant → ㄴ 적이, with final consonant → 은 적이).
How to make ~아/어 보다 — vowel rule (last stem vowel decides 아/어). The auxiliary verb 보다 (lit. see) = try.
Compare the two patterns — both express experience, but the nuance differs.

What is '~아/어 주다'? Attached to a verb stem, it means to do [verb] for someone (else). The action benefits another person. The same ending you saw in Grammar 11 (~아/어 주세요 = please do for me), now mostly used in the past about someone else.
How to form '~아/어 주다' — same vowel rule as past tense. Look at the stem's last vowel.
Recipient marker — who is the action done for? Use 에게 for an animate being. When the recipient is the direct object of the verb itself (helped HIM, waited for ME), use the object marker 을/를 instead.
Past tense: ~아/어 주었습니다. The auxiliary 주다 takes the past ending — the main verb keeps its 아/어 form.
The most basic sentence in Korean is Subject + Predicate. In English, the predicate is a verb, but in Korean both verbs and adjectives can be predicates.
This is the simplest sentence structure in Korean.
What is '~아/어지다'? Attached to an adjective stem, it expresses a change of state — to become / get [adjective].
How to form '~아/어지다' — the ending depends on the last vowel of the adjective stem.
ㅂ-irregular adjectives — adjectives like 덥다 / 춥다 change ㅂ to 우 before the vowel ending, and 우 + 어 contracts to 워.
Past tense: ~아/어졌습니다. The auxiliary 지다 takes the past ending.

What is '~게 되다'? Attached to a verb or adjective stem, it expresses a change brought about by time, circumstance, or external influence — to come to / end up [verb-ing]. Used for life situations and outcomes outside one's direct control.
How to form '~게 되다' — no vowel rule. Always attach 게 되다 directly to the stem.
Grammar 14 vs Grammar 15 — both express change, but apply to different things.
Common usage patterns — ~게 되다 frequently expresses outcomes the speaker did not fully choose.

Why so many guess expressions in Korean? Throughout its long history, Korea experienced numerous wars and invasions. In such uncertain times, people learned to avoid speaking in definitive, absolute terms — preferring softer, more indirect expressions.
What is '~것 같다'? Attached to a verb or adjective stem via a tense-specific modifier, it expresses a guess or supposition — it seems / I think. The modifier changes by tense.
Tense modifier table — choose the modifier that matches when the action happened.

What is '~겠다'? Attached directly to a verb or adjective stem (no vowel rule), it expresses empathy, strong intention, or prediction — three distinct meanings sharing the same form. Context tells you which.
Three meanings, one form — the same ~겠다 works as empathy (toward others), strong determination (about oneself), or weather/news prediction.
This unit focuses on Empathy (① above) — guessing what another person feels based on their situation. The Korean speaker observes and gently guesses to show care.
In Korean, the most basic sentences follow one of three patterns. We have already learned the first two — Structure 3 introduces a new element: the object (목적어).
Many Korean verbs are made of noun + 하다 (do) — for example, 운동하다 (exercise), 청소하다 (clean), 공부하다 (study). These noun + 하다 verbs can be rewritten as noun + 을/를 하다.
한국어에는 명사 + 하다 동사들이 많이 있습니다 — 예: 운동하다, 청소하다, 공부하다. 명사 + 하다 동사는 명사 + 을/를 하다로 바꿔 쓸 수 있습니다.
What is '~네요'? Attached directly to a verb or adjective stem (no vowel rule), it expresses surprise, exclamation, or a fresh discovery — Wow, ...! / Oh, ...! Used when you just noticed something.
Formation table — works on adjectives, verbs, and past forms identically. No irregular rules.
The emotional flavor — '~네요' is a soft, intimate ending showing genuine reaction. Often paired with intensity adverbs like 참 (really) or 정말 (truly) to amplify the feeling.

Why so many tag-question expressions in Korean? Korean culture values soft, indirect speech. Rather than declaring something definitively, speakers often invite the listener to confirm — softening the assertion and seeking shared understanding.

What is '~지요/죠'? Attached directly to a verb/adjective stem (or noun + 이다 stem), it expresses confirmation or seeks soft agreement — the equivalent of English tag questions right? / isn't it? / aren't you?.
Tense and stem-type table — choose the form that matches the tense and the stem.
~지요 vs ~죠 — two forms of the same expression. ~지요 is the formal full form. ~죠 is the everyday spoken short form. Both convey the same meaning and politeness — choose freely depending on the register.

Why so much politeness in Korean? Korean culture places strong emphasis on respect for the listener. Direct commands sound rude, so Koreans wrap requests in honorific endings to soften them. ~(으)세요 is the everyday polite ending — used for commands, requests, greetings, and well-wishes.
Pattern: stem + (으)세요. Attached to a verb or adjective stem, it produces a soft, polite imperative. Choose 세요 or 으세요 based on the stem's final consonant (받침).
받침 X / O 룰 — examples by stem type.
Korean has two ways to use 때 — one for nouns (during), one for verbs/adjectives (when).
How to make Verb/Adj + ~ㄹ/을 때 — 받침 rule (no final consonant → ㄹ 때, with final consonant → 을 때).
Common nouns used with 때 — events, life stages, school/work periods.
Word order: the 때 clause comes BEFORE the main action.
Korean has two ways to use 동안 — one for time-length nouns (for), one for verbs (while).
때 vs 동안 — different focus on time.
Time duration nouns commonly used with 동안.
Verb + ~는 동안 — always 는! No 받침 rule needed.
Korean has two ways to say before — one for nouns (before [noun]), one for verbs (before [verb]-ing).
How to make Verb + ~기 전에 — always 기, regardless of 받침 (no final-consonant rule).
Common nouns used with 전에 — events, life stages, school/work periods.
Korean has two ways to say after — one for nouns (after [noun]), one for verbs (after [verb]-ing).
How to make Verb + ~ㄴ/은 후에 — 받침 rule (no final consonant → ㄴ 후에, with final consonant → 은 후에).
Common nouns used with 후에 — events, daily life, school/work periods.
Compare 전에 vs 후에 — same word order, opposite time direction.
Use ~고 to connect two (or more) actions in sequence — and / and then.
How to make ~고 — always 고, regardless of 받침. No final-consonant rule!
Tense placement changes meaning — putting tense only on the final clause = sequential chain. Putting tense on every clause is not wrong, but the meaning shifts to independent, separate events.
Compare ~고 with ~아/어서 — both connect actions, but their meaning differs.
Use ~아/어서 when the first action is required for the second to happen — without the first, the second cannot occur.
How to make ~아/어서 — vowel rule (last stem vowel decides 아/어).
동사·형용사 + 아/어서 — both verbs and adjectives can attach to ~아/어서. The first clause must be in present (dictionary) form; only the FINAL clause carries tense.
Compare ~고 vs ~아/어서 — both connect actions, but the relation differs.
Use ~(으)면서 when one subject does two actions at the same time.
How to make ~(으)면서 — 받침 rule (no final consonant → 면서, with final consonant → 으면서).
Compare ~(으)면서 vs ~는 동안 — both mean while, but the subject is different.
~(이)면서 with nouns — is both A and B (at once).
Use ~자마자 when one action happens immediately right after another — as soon as.
How to make ~자마자 — always 자마자, regardless of 받침. No final-consonant rule!
Tense goes only on the FINAL clause — the ~자마자 clause stays in stem form even for past events.
Compare ~자마자 with ~고 / ~아/어서 — all sequence actions, but the time gap differs.
Use ~다가 when something happens (often unexpectedly) during/in the middle of another action.
How to make ~다가 — always 다가, regardless of 받침. No final-consonant rule!
Compare ~(으)면서 vs ~다가 — both mean while, but the nuance differs.
Korean has two ways to mark from … to … — one for time, one for place.
부터 alone = starting from / since (no end point).
까지 alone = until / up to — only the end point.
에서 vs 부터 — common confusion. 에서 = place starting point; 부터 = time/order starting point.
Use 덕분에 when something positive happened thanks to a person or thing — expresses gratitude.
Compare 때문에 / 덕분에 / 탓에 — all mean because of, but the speaker's emotion differs.
Verb form: ~ㄴ/은 덕분에 — attaches to verbs after a 받침 rule similar to ~ㄴ/은 후에.
Pattern: stem + 아/어/해서. Vowel harmony decides 아 vs 어; 하다 verbs become 해서.
Vowel harmony examples — by stem-final vowel.
Critical rule — present tense ONLY! The verb/adjective before ~아/어서 must be in present form, even if the meaning is past.
~아/어서 vs ~(으)니까 — Korean splits cause-connector by sentence type.
The most common Korean sentence structure is:

Words expressing time, place, reason, purpose, condition ... are called adverbials (부사어). Adverbials go between the subject and the object. (To emphasize, an adverbial can also come before the subject.)
In beginner Korean, the most common adverbials are time and place.
Time: attach 에 to a time noun.
Place: attach 에서 to a place noun.
Why a second cause connector? While ~아/어서 works only with statements, ~(으)니까 can pair with commands, suggestions, and requests. It also handles past-tense causes — a feature ~아/어서 cannot.
Pattern: stem + (으)니까. Choose 니까 or 으니까 based on the stem-final consonant.
Discovery use of ~(으)니까. Beyond cause, ~(으)니까 also marks a realization — upon doing X, (I found that) Y. Common with verbs of investigation (열다, 보다, 가다).

How does Korean express purpose? Korean has multiple purpose connectors: 위해서 (for, especially with noun targets), ~(으)려고 (in order to, with verb), ~(으)러 가다 (go to do something). Each carries subtle nuance.
Pattern: noun + 을/를 + 위해서. With a verb, use verb stem + 기 위해서 (nominalizer ~기).
~기 위해서 with verbs. To use a verb as the purpose-target, nominalize with ~기.
What is ~(으)려고? Where 위해서 attaches to noun targets, ~(으)려고 attaches to verb stems to express concrete intent — in order to do X.
Pattern: verb stem + (으)려고. Choose 려고 or 으려고 based on stem-final consonant.
Negative form: ~지 않으려고. To express in order NOT to do X, combine ~지 않다 (not to do) with ~려고.
위해서 vs ~(으)려고 — quick rule of thumb: noun-purpose → 위해서; verb-action-purpose → ~(으)려고. Both can often be substituted, but ~(으)려고 emphasizes the speaker's intent more concretely.
Answering 왜 (why) — two ways: a 왜 question can be answered with either purpose (~(으)려고 / 위해서) or cause (~아/어서 / 때문에). Same question, two valid answer types.
~(으)려고 가다 = ~(으)러 가다. With motion verbs (가다 / 오다 / 다니다), the two purpose connectors are interchangeable. With non-motion result verbs, only ~(으)려고 works.
Pattern: verb stem + (으)러 + 가다/오다/다니다. Choose 러 or 으러 based on stem-final consonant.
Common patterns with ~(으)러 가다: errands, appointments, daily activities. Korean speakers use this very frequently in everyday conversation.

~(으)면 = hypothesis or condition. Korean's ~(으)면 covers two related but distinct uses: 1) 가정 (hypothesis) — an imagined situation that may or may not happen. 2) 조건 (condition) — a requirement that triggers a fixed/predictable result.
Pattern: stem + (으)면. Choose 면 or 으면 based on the stem's final consonant. Works with verbs, adjectives, and noun + 이다 stems.
If you want to / In order to (necessary condition)
What is ~(으)려면? A specialized conditional connector — if you want to or "in order to." Almost always paired with ~아/어야 하다 (must do) in the result clause to express a necessary condition.
Pattern: verb stem + (으)려면 + . + 아/어야 하다. Etymology: ~(으)려고 하다 (intend to) + 면 (if) → ~(으)려면 (if you intend to).
~아/어야 하다 — must. The result clause uses this construction. Choose 아야 or 어야 by vowel harmony, then add 하다/되다 (both mean must).
What is ~아/어도? Means even though or even if. Use it when the result is different from what you'd expect from the cause — the cause happens, but the expected result doesn't follow.
Pattern: stem + 아/어/해도. Same vowel-harmony rules as ~아/어서: ㅏ/ㅗ → 아도, other → 어도, 하다 → 해도. Often paired with 아무리 (no matter how) for emphasis.
아무리 ~ 아/어도 — adds "no matter how much" for stronger emphasis. The adverb 아무리 intensifies the cause: even pushing it to the maximum, the expected result still doesn't happen.
~아/어도 vs ~지만. Both can express contrast, but with different focus: ~아/어도 emphasizes despite the cause, result is X; ~지만 just lists two contrasting facts.
~는데/~ㄴ데 sets up the background or situation in the first clause; the second clause shows what happens in that situation.
Adjective: 받침 X → stem + ㄴ데; 받침 O → stem + 은데. Noun + 이다 → noun + 인데.
Comparative — comparing two items. Three core markers based on the relationship.
Superlative — 가장 / 제일. Two patterns chosen by the scope noun's type.
Why nominalize? Verbs and adjectives normally act as predicates. To move them into the subject or object slot, convert them into noun forms.
Three ways to nominalize — pattern by ending.
How to choose — three nominalizers have distinct uses.
Why adverbialize? Verbs and adjectives normally act as predicates. To move them into the adverb slot (modifying the predicate), convert them into adverb forms.
~게 is the most common, productive adverbializer — works with almost every adjective.
~히 applies to many ~하다 adjectives — often interchangeable with ~하게.
~이 is the legacy adverbializer — used in fixed words; 깨끗이 ✅, NOT 깨끗히 ❌.
~아/어하다 turns a feeling adjective into a transitive verb — to feel/show that adjective toward something.
Adjective vs Verb — different particle. The particle on the noun depends on what predicate follows.
~만 = only X. Attach to noun, particle, or place — meaning shifts with position.
~밖에 = nothing except X — must pair with a NEGATIVE predicate (없다 / 안 / 못).
Position changes meaning — attach ~만 to whichever element you want to limit.
~로/으로 attaches to nouns: 받침 X (or ㄹ받침) → ~로; 받침 O → ~으로.
Six core meanings: direction, means/transport, material, cause, role/identity, change/result.
Frequency adverbs go BEFORE the verb (Korean SOV order): 항상 always · 자주 often · 가끔 sometimes.
~마다 attaches to a time noun → every X: 아침마다 (every morning), 날마다 (every day).
Korean uses classifiers after numbers — different nouns require different counters.
Numbers 1-4 take special counter forms — 한 / 두 / 세 / 네 (not 하나/둘/셋/넷).
Most common classifiers — memorize by category (people, animals, books, drinks, vehicles, flat items).
Subject + Indirect Obj + Direct Obj + Verb
Subject + Indirect Obj + Direct Obj + Verb (문장 41~50)
Subject + Indirect Obj + Direct Obj + Verb
But WHO received the gift? The receiver is missing!
Add the receiver — the indirect object — with 에게:
'에게' or '에'? It depends on whether the receiver is animate (a living thing that can move) or not.
When a verb stem ends in ㄹ, that ㄹ drops before endings starting with ㄴ / ㅂ / ㅅ / 오.
Common ㄹ verbs: 알다 (know), 만들다 (make), 팔다 (sell), 놀다 (play), 살다 (live).
When a verb/adjective stem ends in ㅡ, the ㅡ drops before ~아/어 endings.
Vowel choice (아 vs 어) is decided by the vowel BEFORE the dropped ㅡ.
When a verb/adjective stem ends in 르, attaching ~아/어 drops the ㅡ and adds another ㄹ.
Vowel harmony decides 아 vs 어 — based on the vowel BEFORE 르.
Final ㅂ is a hard consonant. When it meets a soft vowel ending (~아/어), the harsh ㅂ softens into a vowel — usually 우, but 오 for 돕다·곱다.
Common ㅂ-irregular: 덥다, 춥다, 돕다, 맵다, 쉽다, 어렵다, 아름답다.
Exceptions — REGULAR ㅂ verbs! 입다 (wear), 잡다 (catch), 좁다 (narrow) — ㅂ stays unchanged.
Final ㄷ is a hard consonant. When it meets a vowel ending (e.g. ~았/었습니다), the harsh ㄷ softens into the smooth liquid ㄹ.
Exceptions — REGULAR ㄷ verbs! 닫다 (close), 받다 (receive), 믿다 (trust) — ㄷ stays unchanged.
Some ㅅ-final verbs drop ㅅ before vowel endings — irregular.
Common ㅅ-irregular: 짓다 (build/cook rice), 낫다 (recover/be better), 붓다 (pour), 긋다 (draw a line).
What is it? Indirect speech is how you retell what someone said — in your own voice, without quotation marks. Like English "He said that …" but with a Korean ending.
How to convert? Drop quotation marks, change the ending to a sentence-type-specific marker, then attach 하다 (say / tell / ask).
Indirect endings depend on the sentence type of the original quote.
Pronoun shift: in indirect speech, 저 (I) becomes 자신 (oneself / himself) — to disambiguate the speaker from a third person.
What is 관형어 (modifier)? A word or phrase that describes a noun — telling us which one or what kind. Like English "red apple" or "the book I read".
Korean places the modifier BEFORE the noun — opposite of English.
Three tense forms attached to the verb stem before the noun.

What is 존댓말? Korean shows respect through built-in grammar — special particles, verb endings, and vocabulary used when speaking about or to someone socially superior (parents, elders, teachers, bosses, strangers).
주체존대 (Subject Honorific): when the subject is socially superior, use subject particle 께서, and add verb infix ~(으)시.
Honorific particles: when the subject or recipient is an elder, the particle itself changes form. Pair these with verb honorific endings for full respect.
Special honorific verbs replace the regular verb entirely. Two groups: subject honorific and object honorific (humble form when acting toward an elder).
Honorific nouns also exist — use them when referring to an elder's belongings or family.
Korean sentence structures — four basic patterns:
Adverbials (부사어) include: time, place, reason, purpose, condition / assumption, background / situation, concession, and more.
In beginner Korean, the most common adverbials are time and place.
Question words (의문대명사 / 의문부사): 누구 (who), 언제 (when), 어디 (where), 무엇 (what), 어떻게 (how), 왜 (why).
For now we focus on 누구, 언제, 어디, 무엇. 어떻게 and 왜 come later.

Position rule: a question word goes in the position of the role it plays — subject in the subject position, time in the time position, place in the place position, object in the object position, predicate in the predicate position.

Korean has two number systems. Sino-Korean (from Chinese) is used for dates, money, phone numbers, and counting above 100. Native Korean is used for hours of the day, age, and counting things up to 99.
Colors are common 어떤 (what kind) answers — for example, What color do you like? → I like blue.
Korean has TWO types of connectors:
A = B in Korean is expressed as A는 B이다 (A is B).
'이다' is a SPECIAL verb. It is a verb, but it attaches DIRECTLY to a noun (no space).
'이다' tenses — formal forms for present, past, future.
New word: '제' = my (humble). It is the shortened form of '저의'.
Present progressive (be V-ing) in Korean is expressed as verb stem + 고 있다.
'~고 있다' is the EASIEST grammar. It has NO irregular forms — no vowel checking, no contraction. Just stem + '고 있습니다'. Always!
Three ways to say is doing — '~고 있다' for verbs, '~중이다' for nouns, and '~는 중이다' for verbs (more emphatic).
Time words used WITHOUT '에': some time words go directly into the time slot (no time marker).
저 (no final consonant) + topic marker 는

Korean adjective 많다 means be many/much. Adverbial form 많이 means a lot, plentifully

Her skin = 그녀의 피부가. 그녀의 피부가 좋아졌습니다 = 그녀는 피부가 좋아졌습니다 — Koreans usually use this second form
좋아지다 (= become better) + past 었습니다 = has become good / improved
Korean order: Subject + Predicate 한국어 어순: 주어 + 서술어
His health = 그의 건강이. 그의 건강이 나빠졌습니다 = 그는 건강이 나빠졌습니다 — Koreans usually use this second form
나쁘다 (ㅡ-irregular: 나쁘 + 아 → 나빠) + 지다 + past 었습니다 = became bad
Korean order: Subject + Predicate 한국어 어순: 주어 + 서술어
저 (no final consonant) + topic marker 는
케이팝 (final consonant ㅂ) + object marker 을
좋아하다 (like, stem 좋아하) + 게 되다 + past 었습니다 = came to like / ended up liking — a gradual change
Korean order: Subject + Object + Verb 한국어 어순: 주어 + 목적어 + 동사
I came to understand Korean culture.
저 (no final consonant) + topic marker 는
한국 문화 (no final consonant on 화) + object marker 를
이해하다 (understand, stem 이해하) + 게 되다 + past 었습니다 = came to understand — a gained insight over time
Korean order: Subject + Object + Verb 한국어 어순: 주어 + 목적어 + 동사
저 (no final consonant) + topic marker 는
병원 + direction marker 에 — destination of admission. 입원하다 takes 에 (entry into a place), not 에서
입원하다 (be hospitalized, stem 입원하) + 게 되다 + past 었습니다 = ended up hospitalized — typically an unintended outcome
Korean order: Subject + Place + Verb 한국어 어순: 주어 + 장소 + 동사
I came to work at another company.
저 (no final consonant) + topic marker 는
다른 (another) + 회사 + place marker 에서 — location where the action (work) happens
일하다 (work, stem 일하) + 게 되다 + past 었습니다 = came to work — outcome from external circumstances (e.g., job change)
Korean order: Subject + Place + Verb 한국어 어순: 주어 + 장소 + 동사
It seems like it rained last night.
어젯밤 + time marker 에 — when the action happened
비 (no final consonant) + subject marker 가
오다 (rain, stem 오) + past modifier ㄴ → 온 + 것 같습니다 — past guess seems like it rained
Korean order: Subject + Time + Predicate (guess, past) 한국어 어순: 주어 + 시간 + 서술어 (guess, past)
It seems like it's raining now.
지금 — used WITHOUT a particle (a bare time adverb)
오다 (rain, stem 오) + present modifier 는 → 오는 + 것 같습니다 — present guess about an ongoing action
Korean order: Subject + Time + Predicate (guess, present) 한국어 어순: 주어 + 시간 + 서술어 (guess, present)
It looks like it will rain tomorrow.
내일 — bare time adverb (no particle, like 지금 / 어제 / 오늘)
오다 (rain, stem 오) + future modifier ㄹ → 올 + 것 같습니다 — future guess looks like it will rain
Korean order: Subject + Time + Predicate (guess, future) 한국어 어순: 주어 + 시간 + 서술어 (guess, future)
I think this exam will be difficult.
이번 시험 (받침 ㅁ) + subject marker 이
어렵다 (difficult — adjective, stem 어렵, ㅂ-irregular) + future modifier 을 → 어려울 + 것 같습니다 — adjective future guess
Korean order: Subject + Predicate (guess, adjective future) 한국어 어순: 주어 + 서술어 (guess, adjective future)
📝 ㅂ-irregular note: 어렵다 stem 어렵 + 을 → 어려울 (ㅂ → 우 + 을 = 울). For 받침 ㅂ adjectives, the future modifier is irregular.
I think the movie will be fun.
그 영화 (no final consonant on 화) + topic marker 는 — focus on the movie as the topic
재미있다 (fun — adjective, stem 재미있, 받침 ㅆ) + future modifier 을 → 재미있을 + 것 같습니다 — adjective future guess
Korean order: Subject + Predicate (guess, adjective future) 한국어 어순: 주어 + 서술어 (guess, adjective future)
기분 (final consonant ㄴ) + subject marker 이
좋다 (good — adjective, stem 좋) + 겠습니다 = empathic guess must feel good
Korean order: Subject + Predicate (empathy) 한국어 어순: 주어 + 서술어 (empathy)
마음 (final consonant ㅁ) + subject marker 이
아프다 (hurt — adjective, stem 아프) + 겠습니다 = empathic guess must be hurting / heartbreaking
Korean order: Subject + Predicate (empathy) 한국어 어순: 주어 + 서술어 (empathy)
배 (no final consonant) + subject marker 가
고프다 (be empty/hungry — adjective, stem 고프) + 겠습니다 = empathic guess must be hungry. The expression 배가 고프다 idiomatically means hungry
Korean order: Subject + Predicate (empathy) 한국어 어순: 주어 + 서술어 (empathy)
기쁘다 (happy — adjective, stem 기쁘) + 겠습니다 = empathic guess must be happy. The Korean speaker often drops the subject (you / 당신은) when context is clear
Korean order: Predicate (empathy, subject implicit) 한국어 어순: 서술어 (empathy, subject implicit)
📝 주어 생략: 한국어에서는 화자·청자가 명확한 일상 대화에서 주어를 자주 생략합니다. 영어 You → 한국어는 (당신은) 기쁘겠습니다에서 당신은 생략 자연스러움.
걱정 (final consonant ㅇ) + subject marker 이
많다 (many — adjective, stem 많) + 겠습니다 = empathic guess must be a lot. The phrase 걱정이 많다 idiomatically means worried
Korean order: Subject + Predicate (empathy) 한국어 어순: 주어 + 서술어 (empathy)
꽃 (final consonant ㅊ) + subject marker 이
참 — intensity adverb really / truly, placed before predicate
예쁘다 (pretty — adjective, stem 예쁘) + 네요 = exclamation are beautiful!
Korean order: Subject + Adverb + Predicate (exclamation) 한국어 어순: 주어 + 부사 + 서술어 (감탄)
경치 (no final consonant) + subject marker 가
좋다 (good — adjective, stem 좋) + 네요 = exclamation
Korean order: Subject + Adverb + Predicate (exclamation) 한국어 어순: 주어 + 부사 + 서술어 (감탄)
날씨 (no final consonant) + subject marker 가. Korean idiomatically uses 날씨 as the subject for today is cold
춥다 (cold — adjective, stem 춥) + 네요 = exclamation is cold!
Korean order: Subject + Adverb + Predicate (exclamation) 한국어 어순: 주어 + 부사 + 서술어 (감탄)
이 음식 (final consonant ㄱ) + topic marker 은 — focus on this specific food
Korean order: Subject + Adverb + Predicate (exclamation) 한국어 어순: 주어 + 부사 + 서술어 (감탄)
방 (final consonant ㅇ) + subject marker 이
Korean order: Subject + Adverb + Predicate (exclamation) 한국어 어순: 주어 + 부사 + 서술어 (감탄)
가격 (final consonant ㄱ) + subject marker 이
Korean order: Subject + Adverb + Predicate (exclamation) 한국어 어순: 주어 + 부사 + 서술어 (감탄)
눈 (snow, 받침 ㄴ) + subject marker 이. Korean realizes the dummy It of It is snowing as the literal subject 눈이
오다 - 오 + 네요 = 오네요. The phrase 눈이 오다 idiomatically means it is snowing
많이 — quantity adverb (from 많다 + 이 ending)
Korean order: Subject + Adverb + Adverb + Verb (exclamation) 한국어 어순: 주어 + 부사 + 부사 + 동사 (감탄)
📝 첫 동사 사례: 223~228은 형용사 + ~네요. 이 문제부터 동사 + ~네요가 등장 — 형성 방식은 동일 (어간 그대로 + 네요).
당신 (final consonant ㄴ) + topic marker 은
노래 (no final consonant) + object marker 를
잘하다 (do well — verb, stem 잘하) + 네요. The phrase 노래를 잘하다 means sing well
Korean order: Subject + Adverb + Object + Verb (exclamation) 한국어 어순: 주어 + 부사 + 목적어 + 동사 (감탄)
A big restaurant opened over there.
크다 (big) → modifier form 큰 + 식당 (final consonant ㅇ) + subject marker 이
생기다 (appear/open — verb, stem 생기) + past 었 → 생겼 + 네요 = exclamation opened!
저기 + place marker 에 — location where the restaurant appeared
Korean order: Subject + Place + Verb (exclamation, past) 한국어 어순: 주어 + 장소 + 동사 (exclamation, past)
📝 과거 + 네요: 과거형 었/았 뒤에도 ~네요 가능. 막 발견한 과거 사실 표현 — 있었네요! 갔네요! 등.
The weather is really nice today, isn't it?
오늘 — bare time adverb (no particle, like 지금, 어제, 내일)
날씨 (no final consonant) + subject marker 가
참 — adverb meaning really / quite (synonyms: 정말, 아주)
좋다 (nice — adjective, stem 좋) + confirmation 죠? → 좋죠? — present-tense soft confirmation
Korean order: Subject + Time + Adverb + Predicate (confirmation, adjective) 한국어 어순: 주어 + 시간 + 부사 + 서술어 (confirmation, adjective)
This coffee is really good, isn't it?
이 (this) + 커피 — subject marker 가 often dropped in casual speech (Korean omits the subject marker for natural flow)
맛있다 (delicious — adjective, stem 맛있) + confirmation 죠? → 맛있죠?
Korean order: Subject + Adverb + Predicate (confirmation, adjective) 한국어 어순: 주어 + 부사 + 서술어 (confirmation, adjective)
The class is at 11 tomorrow, right?
수업 (final consonant ㅂ) + subject marker 이
Noun 11시 (vowel ending — 시 ends with ㅣ) + confirmation 죠? → 11시죠? — noun confirmation, no 이 needed because no final consonant
Korean order: Subject + Time + Predicate (confirmation, noun) 한국어 어순: 주어 + 시간 + 서술어 (confirmation, noun)
📝 명사 + (이)죠 룰: 받침 X (모음 끝) → 명사 + 죠?. 받침 O (자음 끝) → 명사 + 이죠?. 예: 학생이죠? (학생 has final consonant ㅇ) vs 11시죠? (시 has no final consonant).
You were really tired yesterday, weren't you?
너무 — adverb meaning really / too much (often emphatic)
피곤하다 (tired — adjective, stem 피곤하) + past ~였 → 피곤했 + confirmation 죠? → 피곤했죠?. Korean omits the subject 당신은 — context implies the listener
Korean order: Time + Adverb + Predicate (confirmation, adjective past, subject omitted in Korean) 한국어 어순: 시간 + 부사 + 서술어 (confirmation, adjective past, subject omitted in Korean)
📝 Past + ~죠? 형성: stem + 았/었 + 죠?. 모음 ㅏ/ㅗ → 았, 그 외 → 었. 피곤하다 → 하 + 였 → 했 (하다 동사·형용사 패턴) + 죠? = 피곤했죠?.
This song is really good, isn't it?
이 (this) + 노래 — subject marker often dropped in casual speech
좋다 (good — adjective, stem 좋) + confirmation 죠? → 좋죠?
Korean order: Subject + Adverb + Predicate (confirmation, adjective) 한국어 어순: 주어 + 부사 + 서술어 (confirmation, adjective)
안녕하다 (be peaceful — adjective, stem 안녕하) + polite ending 세요? (받침 X) → 안녕하세요? — Korean's all-purpose greeting (literally are you peaceful?)
Korean order: Predicate (greeting, polite question) 한국어 어순: 서술어 (greeting, polite question)
어서 — adverb meaning quickly / right away. Common in welcome greetings
오다 (come — verb, stem 오, 받침 X) + polite ending 세요 → 오세요. Idiomatic 어서 오세요 = welcome (literally please come quickly)
Korean order: Adverb + Predicate (welcome greeting) 한국어 어순: 부사 + 서술어 (welcome greeting)
여기 (here) + position marker 에. Position verbs (앉다, 눕다, 서다) use ~에 (not ~에서)
앉다 (sit — verb, stem 앉, 받침 ㅈ) + polite ending 으세요 (받침 O → 으세요) → 앉으세요
Korean order: Place + Predicate (polite imperative) 한국어 어순: 장소 + 서술어 (polite imperative)
📝 Place + 에 vs 에서: Position verbs (앉다, 눕다, 서다, 있다) and direction verbs (가다, 오다) use ~에. Action verbs (먹다, 공부하다) use ~에서. So: 여기에 앉으세요 ✅ vs 여기에서 먹으세요 ✅.
비빔밥 — object marker often dropped in restaurant ordering for natural flow
하나 — native Korean number one (used for counting items: 하나, 둘, 셋...)
주다 (give — verb, stem 주, 받침 X) + polite ending 세요 → 주세요. ~ 주세요 = please give me ~ (idiomatic ordering pattern)
Korean order: Object + Quantity + Predicate (polite request) 한국어 어순: 목적어 + Quantity + 서술어 (polite request)
맛있다 (delicious — adjective, stem 맛있) + adverbial ending 게 → 맛있게 (deliciously)
Honorific verb 드시다 (eat — honorific of 먹다) + polite ending 세요 → 드세요. Idiomatic 맛있게 드세요 = Enjoy your meal (literally please eat deliciously)
Korean order: Adverb + Predicate (idiomatic, honorific) 한국어 어순: 부사 + 서술어 (idiomatic, honorific)
📝 Idiom note: Direct translation 당신의 식사를 즐기세요 (please enjoy your meal) is grammatically correct but unnatural. Korean speakers always use 맛있게 드세요 — memorize this as a complete idiom.
안녕히 — adverb form of 안녕 (peace) — meaning peacefully
Honorific verb 가시다 (go — honorific of 가다) + polite ending 세요 → 가세요. Said to the person who is leaving (literally please go peacefully)
Korean order: Adverb + Predicate (farewell, to person leaving) 한국어 어순: 부사 + 서술어 (farewell, to person leaving)
Honorific verb 계시다 (stay — honorific of 있다) + polite ending 세요 → 계세요. Said to the person who is staying (literally please stay peacefully)
Korean order: Adverb + Predicate (farewell, to person staying) 한국어 어순: 부사 + 서술어 (farewell, to person staying)

📝 가세요 vs 계세요 핵심 룰: Both mean Goodbye. Used based on who is leaving and who is staying. • You stay, friend leaves → say 안녕히 가세요 (please go peacefully). • You leave, friend stays → say 안녕히 계세요 (please stay peacefully). • Both leave (different directions) → both say 안녕히 가세요 to each other.
좋다 (good — adjective, stem 좋) + present-modifier 은 → 좋은 (good, modifying noun) + 하루 (day) = a good day
되다 (become — verb, stem 되, 받침 X) + polite ending 세요 → 되세요. Idiomatic 좋은 하루 되세요 = Have a nice day (literally please become a nice day)
Korean order: Object + Predicate (polite wish) 한국어 어순: 목적어 + 서술어 (polite wish)
행복하다 (be happy — adjective, stem 행복하) + polite ending 세요 (받침 X) → 행복하세요. Adjective + (으)세요 conveys a wish (please be happy)
Korean order: Adverb + Predicate (polite wish, adjective) 한국어 어순: 부사 + 서술어 (polite wish, adjective)
건강하다 (be healthy — adjective, stem 건강하) + polite ending 세요 → 건강하세요. Common in farewells, especially to elders or people you care about
Korean order: Adverb + Predicate (polite wish, adjective) 한국어 어순: 부사 + 서술어 (polite wish, adjective)
새 (new) + 해 (year) = 새해. Bare time noun (no particle)
복 — noun meaning fortune / luck. Object marker 을 dropped in idiomatic phrasing
많다 (be many — adjective, stem 많) + adverbial ending 이 → 많이 (a lot, abundantly)
받다 (receive — verb, stem 받, 받침 ㄷ) + polite ending 으세요 (받침 O → 으세요) → 받으세요
Korean order: Time + Adverb + Object + Predicate (idiomatic, polite wish) 한국어 어순: 시간 + 부사 + 목적어 + 서술어 (idiomatic, polite wish)

📝 Idiom note: Korean people don't translate Happy New Year directly. Instead, they say 새해 복 많이 받으세요 — literally please receive lots of new year fortune. The wish focuses on receiving good fortune, not on being happy. Memorize as a complete idiom.
What did you do during the vacation?
당신 (ends with a final consonant) + topic marker 은
하다 (verb) → 했습니까? (하다 → 했습니다, irregular; interrogative form)
Korean order: Subject + Time + Object + Verb 한국어 어순: 주어 + 시간 + 목적어 + 동사
I went backpacking during the vacation.
배낭여행을 갔다 (went backpacking) = 배낭여행을 했다 = 배낭여행을 했습니다
Korean order: Subject + Time + Object + Verb 한국어 어순: 주어 + 시간 + 목적어 + 동사
He did volunteer work during his vacation.
봉사활동 (ends with a final consonant) + object marker 을
Korean order: Subject + Time + Object + Verb 한국어 어순: 주어 + 시간 + 목적어 + 동사
He listens to music when he studies.
음악 (ends with a final consonant) + object marker 을
공부하다 (verb) → stem 공부하 + ㄹ 때 → 공부할 때 (when studying)
Korean order: Subject + Time + Object + Verb 한국어 어순: 주어 + 시간 + 목적어 + 동사
I sing when I'm in a good mood.
+ . Compound: 노래를 부르다 = "sing a song" = sing (Korean requires 노래를 even when English just says "sing")
when I'm in a good mood
기분이 (mood + subject marker) + 좋다 → "좋 + 을 때" — when (mood) is good
Korean order: Subject + Time + Object + Verb 한국어 어순: 주어 + 시간 + 목적어 + 동사
How long did you live in Korea?
당신 (ends with a final consonant) + topic marker 은
Verb stem 살 + past 았 + interrogative 습니까?
한국 + location marker 에서 (where the living happens)
Korean order: Subject + Time + Place + Verb 한국어 어순: 주어 + 시간 + 장소 + 동사
I lived in Korea for two years.
Verb stem 살 + past 았 + formal 습니다
Korean order: Subject + Time + Place + Verb 한국어 어순: 주어 + 시간 + 장소 + 동사
She studied Korean for three hours.
Korean order: Subject + Time + Object + Verb 한국어 어순: 주어 + 시간 + 목적어 + 동사
He was in the hospital for a week.
병원 + location marker 에 (location of being, with 있다)
Korean order: Subject + Time + Place + Verb 한국어 어순: 주어 + 시간 + 장소 + 동사
I worked part-time during the vacation.
Korean order: Subject + Time + Object + Verb 한국어 어순: 주어 + 시간 + 목적어 + 동사
I couldn't eat anything for two days.
Long-form negation: stem 먹 + 지 못했습니다 (past of ~지 못하다)
아무것 (anything) + emphatic 도 (always paired with negative verb)
Korean order: Subject + Time + Object + Verb 한국어 어순: 주어 + 시간 + 목적어 + 동사
It snowed while we were sleeping.
눈 (snow, ends with a final consonant) + subject marker 이. Korean expresses It snowed as 눈이 왔다 (lit. snow came)
Subordinate clause subject 우리가 (subject marker 가, NOT 는!) + verb stem 자 + 는 동안
Korean order: Subject + Time + Verb 한국어 어순: 주어 + 시간 + 동사
The students cleaned the classroom before class.
학생들 (ends with a final consonant) + topic marker 은
교실 (ends with a final consonant) + object marker 을
Korean order: Subject + Time + Object + Verb 한국어 어순: 주어 + 시간 + 목적어 + 동사
She prepared the materials before the meeting.
Korean order: Subject + Time + Object + Verb 한국어 어순: 주어 + 시간 + 목적어 + 동사
Sumi plans to get TOPIK Level 6 before graduation.
Verb 따다 (get/earn) + ~려고 하다 (plan to, Grammar 8). 따 (no 받침) + 려고 합니다
토픽 6급 (ends with a final consonant) + object marker 을
Korean order: Subject + Time + Object + Verb 한국어 어순: 주어 + 시간 + 목적어 + 동사
She traveled around Southeast Asia before coming to Korea.
한국에 (Korea + direction marker 에) + verb 오다 → 오 + 기 전에 — before coming
Korean order: Subject + Time + Object + Verb 한국어 어순: 주어 + 시간 + 목적어 + 동사
We booked a hotel before going on the trip.
호텔 (ends with a final consonant) + object marker 을
Verb 여행하다 (travel) → 여행하 + 기 전에 — before traveling
Korean order: Subject + Time + Object + Verb 한국어 어순: 주어 + 시간 + 목적어 + 동사
He says a prayer before eating.
Verb stem 하 (do) + ㅂ니다 — does (with 기도 = says a prayer)
Verb 식사하다 (have a meal) → 식사하 + 기 전에 — before eating a meal
Korean order: Subject + Time + Object + Verb 한국어 어순: 주어 + 시간 + 목적어 + 동사
I met my friend after class.
Past form of 만나다 (meet) — met. Stem 만나 + 았 → 만났
Korean order: Subject + Time + Object + Verb 한국어 어순: 주어 + 시간 + 목적어 + 동사
My father drinks coffee after a meal.
Korean order: Subject + Time + Object + Verb 한국어 어순: 주어 + 시간 + 목적어 + 동사
After cleaning, the street was clean.
(used because the street is the new info)
Adjective 깨끗하다 (clean) + 아/어지다 (become) → 깨끗해지 + 었습니다 = became clean
Korean order: Subject + Time + Verb 한국어 어순: 주어 + 시간 + 동사
He took a shower after exercising.
Past form of 하다 — did (with 샤워를 = took a shower)
Verb 운동하다 (exercise) → stem 운동하 (no final consonant) + ㄴ 후에 → 운동한 후에
Korean order: Subject + Time + Object + Verb 한국어 어순: 주어 + 시간 + 목적어 + 동사
She wrote a book report after reading the book.
Past form of 쓰다 (write) — wrote. Stem 쓰 + 었 → 썼
독후감 (ends with a final consonant) + object marker 을
Object 책을 (book + 을) + verb 읽다 → stem 읽 (final consonant ㄺ) + 은 후에 → 읽은 후에
Korean order: Subject + Time + Object + Verb 한국어 어순: 주어 + 시간 + 목적어 + 동사
My father eats breakfast and then drinks coffee.
아침을 먹다 (eat breakfast) = 아침 식사를 하다 = 아침 식사를 하고 (and then)
Korean order: Subject + Object 1 + Verb 1+고 + Object 2 + Verb 2 한국어 어순: 주어 + 목적어1 + 동사1+고 + 목적어2 + 동사2
My father drinks coffee and reads the newspaper.
커피 + 마시다 (drink) → stem 마시 + 고 for sequential and
Korean order: Subject + Object 1 + Verb 1+고 + Object 2 + Verb 2 한국어 어순: 주어 + 목적어1 + 동사1+고 + 목적어2 + 동사2
My father reads the newspaper and then goes to work.
신문 + 보다 (read) → stem 보 + 고 for sequential and then. No tense; final clause carries tense
회사 (workplace) + . Motion verb takes 에 for destination
Korean order: Subject + Object 1 + Verb 1+고 + Place + Verb 2 한국어 어순: 주어 + 목적어1 + 동사1+고 + 장소 + 동사2
She washed her face and then put on makeup.
Idiom 세수를 하다 = wash one's face (lit. "do face-washing"). 하다 → stem 하 + 고 for sequential and then. No past tense here; final clause carries tense
Idiom 화장을 하다 = put on makeup (lit. "do makeup")
Korean order: Subject + Object 1 + Verb 1+고 + Object 2 + Verb 2 한국어 어순: 주어 + 목적어1 + 동사1+고 + 목적어2 + 동사2
He finished work and met his friend.
퇴근하다 (leave the office) → stem 퇴근하 + 고 for sequential and
Korean order: Subject + Verb 1+고 + Object + Verb 2 한국어 어순: 주어 + 동사1+고 + 목적어 + 동사2
He cleaned, did laundry, and met a friend on the weekend.
청소하다 (clean) → stem 청소하 + 고 for sequential and
빨래하다 (do laundry) → stem 빨래하 + 고 for sequential and
Korean order: Subject + Time + Verb 1+고 + Verb 2+고 + Object + Verb 3 한국어 어순: 주어 + 시간 + 동사1+고 + 동사2+고 + 목적어 + 동사3
Sumi went to a café and met her friend.
커피숍 (café) + . ~아서 marks going as the required precondition for meeting (not ~고, which would mean two unrelated actions)
Korean order: Subject + Place + Verb 1+아/어서 + Object + Verb 2 한국어 어순: 주어 + 장소 + 동사1+아/어서 + 목적어 + 동사2
Sumi met her friend and went to the theater.
친구 (friend) + . ~아서 marks meeting as the precondition for going together (not ~고)
극장 (theater) — sentence has motion verb 가다, so the place takes 에 (destination marker)
Korean order: Subject + Object + Verb 1+아/어서 + Place + Verb 2 한국어 어순: 주어 + 목적어 + 동사1+아/어서 + 장소 + 동사2
Sumi went to the theater and watched a movie.
극장 (theater) + . ~아서 marks going as the precondition for watching
Idiom 영화를 보다 = watch a movie (보다 alone = "see"; with 영화를 it specifically means "watch")
Korean order: Subject + Place + Verb 1+아/어서 + Object + Verb 2 한국어 어순: 주어 + 장소 + 동사1+아/어서 + 목적어 + 동사2
Sujae went to the department store and bought a gift.
went to the department store and
백화점 (department store) + . ~아서 marks going as the precondition for buying — Sujae had to be at the store to buy
Korean order: Subject + Place + Verb 1+아/어서 + Object + Verb 2 한국어 어순: 주어 + 장소 + 동사1+아/어서 + 목적어 + 동사2
Sujae bought flowers and gave them to his friend.
꽃 (flowers) + . ~아서 ties the object across — the SAME flowers bought are what he gave (not ~고, which would separate the actions)
친구 (friend) + IO marker 에게 for an animate recipient — Korean drops his
Korean order: Subject + Object + Verb 1+아/어서 + Recipient + Verb 2 한국어 어순: 주어 + 목적어 + 동사1+아/어서 + 받는 사람 + 동사2
She went to the gym and exercised.
체육관 (gym) + . ~아서 marks going as the precondition for exercising
Korean order: Subject + Place + Verb 1+아/어서 + Object + Verb 2 한국어 어순: 주어 + 장소 + 동사1+아/어서 + 목적어 + 동사2
He went to the bank and withdrew money.
은행 (bank) + . ~아서 marks going as the precondition for withdrawing
Idiom 돈을 찾다 = withdraw money (lit. "find/retrieve money" — Korean uses 찾다 idiomatically for bank withdrawal)
Korean order: Subject + Place + Verb 1+아/어서 + Object + Verb 2 한국어 어순: 주어 + 장소 + 동사1+아/어서 + 목적어 + 동사2
I study while listening to music.
듣다 → 듣 + 으면서 (받침 ㄷ + 모음 : ㄷ - ㄹ) → 들으면서
Korean order: Subject + Adverbial (time: ~(으)면서) + Predicate 한국어 어순: 주어 + 부사어 (시간: ~(으)면서) + 서술어
Father eats a meal while watching the news.
Korean order: Subject + Adverbial (time: ~(으)면서) + Predicate 한국어 어순: 주어 + 부사어 (시간: ~(으)면서) + 서술어
Mother cooks while singing a song.
부르다: 1) 노래를 부르다 (sing a song), 2) 이름을 부르다 (call someone's name)
Korean order: Subject + Adverbial (time: ~(으)면서) + Predicate 한국어 어순: 주어 + 부사어 (시간: ~(으)면서) + 서술어
She reads a book while drinking coffee.
Korean order: Subject + Adverbial (time: ~(으)면서) + Object + Predicate 한국어 어순: 주어 + 부사어 (시간: ~(으)면서) + 목적어 + 서술어
Korean order: Subject + Adverbial (time: ~(으)면서) + Predicate 한국어 어순: 주어 + 부사어 (시간: ~(으)면서) + 서술어
You should not call while driving.
Pattern ~(으)면 안 되다 = must not / should not (prohibition, from Grammar 10). Subject 당신 (you) is omitted — general advice
The danger is precisely that these two acts happen at the same time
Korean order: Adverbial (time: ~(으)면서) + Predicate (prohibition) 한국어 어순: 부사어 (시간: ~(으)면서) + 서술어 (금지)
He met many good people while traveling.
Korean order: Subject + Adverbial (time: ~(으)면서) + Object + Predicate 한국어 어순: 주어 + 부사어 (시간: ~(으)면서) + 목적어 + 서술어
He is both a singer and an actor.
Korean order: Subject + Adverbial (time: ~(이)면서) + Predicate 한국어 어순: 주어 + 부사어 (시간: ~(이)면서) + 서술어
He drank water as soon as he woke up.
as soon as he woke up
Korean order: Subject + Adverbial (time: ~자마자) + Object + Predicate 한국어 어순: 주어 + 부사어 (시간: ~자마자) + 목적어 + 서술어
The power went out as soon as I turned on the computer.
나가다 (go out). Pair: 전기가 나가다 (power goes out) / 전기가 들어오다 (power comes back on)
as soon as I turned on the computer
The adverbial clause has its own internal subject (제가) — different from the main subject (전기가). ~자마자 allows this
Korean order: Subject + Predicate + Adverbial (time: ~자마자) 한국어 어순: 주어 + 서술어 + 부사어 (시간: ~자마자)
He checked in as soon as he arrived at the airport.
as soon as he arrived at the airport
Korean order: Subject + Adverbial (time: ~자마자) + Predicate 한국어 어순: 주어 + 부사어 (시간: ~자마자) + 서술어
We parted as soon as we met.
Korean order: Subject + Adverbial (time: ~자마자) + Predicate 한국어 어순: 주어 + 부사어 (시간: ~자마자) + 서술어
She fell asleep while watching TV.
Korean order: Subject + Adverbial (time: ~다가) + Predicate 한국어 어순: 주어 + 부사어 (시간: ~다가) + 서술어
He met a friend while walking on the street.
길을 걷다 → 길을 걷 + 다가 → 길을 걷다가
Korean order: Subject + Adverbial (time: ~다가) + Object + Predicate 한국어 어순: 주어 + 부사어 (시간: ~다가) + 목적어 + 서술어
He broke a dish while washing the dishes.
설거지하다 = 설거지를 하다 → 설거지를 하 + 다가 → 설거지를 하다가
Korean order: Subject + Adverbial (time: ~다가) + Object + Predicate 한국어 어순: 주어 + 부사어 (시간: ~다가) + 목적어 + 서술어
He had a nightmare while sleeping.
꿈을 꾸다 = have a dream (lit. "dream a dream")
잠자다 = 잠을 자다 → 잠을 자 + 다가 → 잠을 자다가
Korean order: Subject + Adverbial (time: ~다가) + Object + Predicate 한국어 어순: 주어 + 부사어 (시간: ~다가) + 목적어 + 서술어
Equivalent forms: 여름방학이 오늘 시작됩니다 (verb form) = 오늘부터 여름방학입니다 (copula form — used in this exercise)
Korean order: Subject + Adverbial + Predicate 한국어 어순: 주어 + 부사어 + 서술어
We have a vacation from this Monday to next Sunday.
from this Monday to next Sunday
Korean order: Adverbial (time: ~부터 ~까지) + Predicate 한국어 어순: 부사어 (시간: ~부터 ~까지) + 서술어
Sumi studied Korean hard from morning to night.
공부하다 → 한국어를 공부하다 → 한국어를 열심히 공부하다 → 한국어를 열심히 공부했습니다
Korean order: Subject + Adverbial (time: ~부터 ~까지) + Object + Predicate 한국어 어순: 주어 + 부사어 (시간: ~부터 ~까지) + 목적어 + 서술어
The exam range is from number 1 to 100.
1번 (number 1) — ~부터 used here for ordinal/numerical range, not just time
Korean order: Subject + Adverbial (range: ~부터 ~까지) + Predicate 한국어 어순: 주어 + 부사어 (범위: ~부터 ~까지) + 서술어
The exam time is from 9 AM to 1 PM.
Korean order: Subject + Adverbial (time: ~부터 ~까지) + Predicate 한국어 어순: 주어 + 부사어 (시간: ~부터 ~까지) + 서술어
It takes three hours from Seoul to Busan by KTX.
Korean order: ~에서 ~까지 + Instrument (~(으)로) + Time + 걸리다 (Predicate) 한국어 어순: ~에서 ~까지 + 도구 (~(으)로) + 시간 + 걸리다 (서술어)
How much is the taxi fare from Incheon Airport to Seoul City Hall?
인천공항 (Incheon Airport) — places use ~에서 for origin
Korean order: Adverbial (place: ~에서 ~까지) + Subject + Predicate 한국어 어순: 부사어 (장소: ~에서 ~까지) + 주어 + 서술어
The airport was paralyzed because of the heavy snow.
Korean order: Adverbial (cause: 때문에) + Subject + Predicate 한국어 어순: 부사어 (원인: 때문에) + 주어 + 서술어
The trip was canceled because of the typhoon.
Korean order: Adverbial (cause: 때문에) + Subject + Predicate 한국어 어순: 부사어 (원인: 때문에) + 주어 + 서술어
There was a car accident because of the fog.
나다: 1) 사고가 나다 (an accident occurs) 2) 불이 나다 (a fire breaks out) 사고가 나다 = 교통사고가 나다
Korean order: Adverbial (cause: 때문에) + Subject + Predicate 한국어 어순: 부사어 (원인: 때문에) + 주어 + 서술어
The road is blocked because of the car accident.
Korean order: Adverbial (cause: 때문에) + Subject + Predicate 한국어 어순: 부사어 (원인: 때문에) + 주어 + 서술어
It is noisy because of the roadwork.
Korean order: Adverbial (cause: 때문에) + Predicate 한국어 어순: 부사어 (원인: 때문에) + 서술어
He got lung cancer because of smoking.
Korean order: Subject + Adverbial (cause: 때문에) + Predicate 한국어 어순: 주어 + 부사어 (원인: 때문에) + 서술어
These days, the economy is bad because of the exchange rate.
경기 (economic conditions, business climate) — cf. 경제 (economy, abstract concept)
Korean order: Adverbial (time + cause: 때문에) + Subject + Predicate 한국어 어순: 부사어 (시간 + 원인: 때문에) + 주어 + 서술어
I passed the TOPIK exam thanks to my teacher.
Korean order: Subject + Adverbial (cause: 덕분에) + Adverbial (place) + Predicate 한국어 어순: 주어 + 부사어 (원인: 덕분에) + 부사어 (장소) + 서술어
The world became convenient thanks to AI.
Korean order: Adverbial (cause: 덕분에) + Subject + Predicate 한국어 어순: 부사어 (원인: 덕분에) + 주어 + 서술어
Korean order: Adverbial (cause: 아/어서) + Predicate 한국어 어순: 부사어 (원인: 아/어서) + 서술어
📝 Idiom: 만나서 반갑습니다 — Korean's standard introduction. Subject 저는 omitted — context implies the speaker.
Korean order: Adverbial (cause: 아/어서) + Predicate 한국어 어순: 부사어 (원인: 아/어서) + 서술어
Sumi has many friends because she has a good personality.
because she has a good personality
Korean order: Subject + Adverbial (cause: 아/어서) + Predicate 한국어 어순: 주어 + 부사어 (원인: 아/어서) + 서술어
Why are you going to the hospital?
Korean order: Subject + Adverbial (cause + place) + Predicate 한국어 어순: 주어 + 부사어 (원인 + 장소) + 서술어
I'm going to the hospital because I have a stomachache.
아프다 → 아프 + 아서 → 아파서 (ㅡ-irregular: ㅡ drops)
Korean order: Subject + Adverbial (cause + place) + Predicate 한국어 어순: 주어 + 부사어 (원인 + 장소) + 서술어
📝 ㅡ-irregular: 아프다 stem 아프 + 아서 → 아ㅍ + 아서 (drop ㅡ) → 아파서. Same rule for 바쁘다 (busy) → 바빠서, 슬프다 (sad) → 슬퍼서.
Korean order: Subject + Adverbial (cause) + Object + Predicate 한국어 어순: 주어 + 부사어 (원인) + 목적어 + 서술어
I study Korean because I like K-pop.
Korean order: Subject + Adverbial (cause) + Object + Predicate 한국어 어순: 주어 + 부사어 (원인) + 목적어 + 서술어
📝 좋다 (adj) vs 좋아하다 (verb): 저는 케이팝이 좋습니다 = 저는 케이팝을 좋아합니다 (same meaning, different particle). Adjective takes 이/가; verb takes 을/를.
The road is blocked, so I think I will be late.
막히다 → 막히 + 어서 → 막혀서 (이 + 어 → 여)
so I think I will be late
늦다 + ~을 것 같다 (future guess) → 늦을 것 같습니다
Korean order: Adverbial (cause) + Predicate 한국어 어순: 부사어 (원인) + 서술어
I didn't know the way, so I took a taxi.
Korean order: Subject + Adverbial (cause) + Object + Predicate 한국어 어순: 주어 + 부사어 (원인) + 목적어 + 서술어
📝 르-irregular: 모르다 stem 모르 + 아서 → 모ㄹ + ㄹ아서 → 몰라서. Same rule for 빠르다 → 빨라서 (fast), 부르다 → 불러서 (call).
Since it's raining a lot, shall we leave work early?
비 + subject 가 + 많이 + 오다 (come/rain — verb, stem 오, 받침 X) + 니까 → 오니까
퇴근하다 (leave work) + suggestion ~ㄹ까요? → 퇴근할까요? — shall we?
Korean order: Cause + Adverb + Predicate 한국어 어순: 원인 + 부사 + 서술어
Since it's raining a lot, let's leave work early.
Same as Q325 — 오다 + 니까 → 오니까
Korean order: Cause + Adverb + Predicate 한국어 어순: 원인 + 부사 + 서술어
Since it's raining a lot, please leave work early.
Korean order: Cause + Adverb + Predicate 한국어 어순: 원인 + 부사 + 서술어
📝 명령 3종 비교: ~ㄹ까요? (shall we?) · ~ㅂ시다 (let's) · ~세요 (please) — 모두 ~(으)니까와 호환. 강도 순: 청유 의문 < 청유 < 정중 명령.
Since it's cold, let's close the window.
춥다 (cold — adjective, stem 춥, 받침 ㅂ, ㅂ-irregular) + 으니까 → 춥 → 추우 (ㅂ → 우 before vowel) → 추우니까
닫다 (close — verb, stem 닫, 받침 ㄷ) + suggestion ~읍시다 → 닫읍시다
Korean order: Cause + Object + Predicate 한국어 어순: 원인 + 목적어 + 서술어
📝 ㅂ-irregular: 춥다 stem 춥 + 으니까 → ㅂ → 우 (모음 앞) → 추우 + 니까 = 추우니까. 같은 패턴: 덥다 → 더우니까 (hot), 어렵다 → 어려우니까 (difficult).
Since it's hot, let's turn on the air conditioner.
덥다 (hot — adjective, stem 덥, ㅂ-irregular) + 으니까 → 더우 + 니까 = 더우니까
켜다 (turn on — verb, stem 켜, 받침 X) + suggestion ~ㅂ시다 → 켭시다
Korean order: Cause + Object + Predicate 한국어 어순: 원인 + 목적어 + 서술어
위험하다 (dangerous — adjective, stem 위험하, 받침 X — 하 ends in 모음 ㅏ) + 니까 → 위험하니까
조심하다 (be careful) + polite ending 세요 → 조심하세요
Korean order: Cause + Predicate 한국어 어순: 원인 + 서술어
When I opened the bag, the passport was not there.
열다 (open — verb, stem 열, ㄹ-irregular) + (으)니까 → ㄹ drops before ㄴ → 여니까. Discovery use: upon opening (I found that).
Korean order: Subject + Object + Discovery + Predicate 한국어 어순: 주어 + 목적어 + Discovery + 서술어
📝 ㄹ-irregular: 열 + 으니까 → ㄹ drops before ㄴ → 여니까. 같은 룰: 살다 → 사니까, 만들다 → 만드니까, 알다 → 아니까.
He quit smoking for his health.

Korean order: Adverbial (conditional: noun + ~이면) + Object + Predicate 한국어 어순: 부사어 (조건: 명사 + ~이면) + 목적어 + 서술어

살이 빠지다 = lose weight → 살이 빠지 + 지 않다 → 살이 빠지지 않다

⚠️ ~기 for subjects, sports, plans: ⚠️ ~기는 과목, 운동 종목, 계획 등에 사용합니다: 과목 (Subjects): 말하다 → 말하기 (speaking) | 듣다 → 듣기 (listening) 읽다 → 읽기 (reading) | 쓰다 → 쓰기 (writing) These are the four language skills used in TOPIK and Korean language tests! 이것은 토픽과 한국어 시험에서 사용하는 4가지 언어 기능입니다! 스포츠 종목 (Sports): 걷기 (walking) | 달리기 (running) 높이 뛰기 (high jump) | 던지기 (throwing) 계획 (Plan): 살 빼기 (losing weight) | 여행하기 (traveling) | 이사하기 (moving)

📅 ~마다 vs 매~ — equivalent "every [time unit]" 시간 단위 명사 4종은 두 가지 방식으로 표현할 수 있습니다 — 고유어(~마다) 또는 한자어(매~). 고유어 ~마다 한자어 매~ English 날마다매일 (每日)every day 주마다매주 (每週)every week 월마다매월 (每月)every month 해마다매년 (每年)every year 두 형태 모두 같은 의미. 한자어 매~가 문어·격식 표현에서 약간 더 흔합니다 (예: 신문·공고). 일상 대화에서는 둘 다 자연스럽게 쓰입니다.

학생 + 네 명 (4 = 네, classifier 명 for people)

⚠️ 이/가 position — two ways, same meaning! ⚠️ 이/가 위치 — 두 가지 방법, 같은 뜻! 학생이 네 명 있습니다. = 학생 네 명이 있습니다. (4 students are in the classroom. — same meaning! 같은 뜻!) 이/가 can go after the noun OR after the classifier — both are correct! 이/가는 명사 뒤에 올 수도 있고 종별사 뒤에 올 수도 있습니다 — 둘 다 맞습니다!

⚠️ Why 자신? — 왜 자신을 사용합니까? In direct speech, the speaker says 저는 (I). 직접화법에서는 말하는 사람이 저라고 합니다. But in indirect speech, 저 changes to 자신 (oneself/himself). 하지만 간접화법에서는 저가 자신 (자기 자신)으로 바뀝니다. 직접화법간접화법 그는 "저는 회사원입니다."라고 했습니다. (He said, "I am an office worker.")그는 자신이 회사원이라고 했습니다. (He said that he was an office worker.) If you use 그 instead of 자신, it is confusing — is it the same person or a different person? 자신 대신 그를 사용하면 같은 사람인지 다른 사람인지 혼동됩니다!
