Why tense is important in Korean
In Korean, the predicate (verb/adjective) comes at the very end of the sentence — and it determines the most important meaning.
Many grammar patterns express predicates, but the most basic and most frequently used are the present, past, and future of verbs/adjectives.
These three tenses are the most fundamental basics of Korean. Understand them well and practice diligently.
Tense is the grammar that expresses time in verbs and adjectives. Korean has three tenses.
In Korean, you use different speech styles depending on the situation, even for the same meaning.
news, presentations, meetings, elders, first meetings
news articles, books, reports, TOPIK II writing
In this textbook, we will learn the formal style (-ㅂ니다/습니다) first.
Because the formal style has the simplest rules and can be used politely in any situation.
What is the Base Form of a Predicate?
When you look up a word in a Korean dictionary, verbs and adjectives always appear in a form ending with '-다'.
This shape is called the 기본형 (base form). It is the standard form found when looking up a word in a Korean dictionary. It is the starting point from which every conjugated form is derived.
The stem (어간) carries the meaning of the word.
Every Korean verb and adjective base form ends in '-다'. The ending '-다' is the dictionary-citation ending; during conjugation it is replaced by other endings to express tense and speech style.
A word ending in '-다' signals that it is the predicate of the sentence — the part that completes the meaning.
In Korean, adjectives conjugate just like verbs. Adjectives also change form to express tense and speech style.
When a word changes its form to fit the sentence, that change is called "conjugation" (활용).
One base form "changes clothes" into many shapes — depending on tense (present / past / future) and style (formal / casual). The meaning stays, only the ending changes.
The front part (stem) stays the same — only the ending (어미) changes. That's how one word expresses many meanings.
Now let's learn how to change the base form into the present, past, and future formal forms.
Remove '-다' and you get the stem.
Check the last letter of the stem
Korean letters are made by combining consonants and vowels.
The consonant at the bottom of a letter is called '받침' (final consonant).
When the final consonant ㅂ meets ㄴ (a softer sound), the ㅂ changes into the softer sound ㅁ. So when reading, you should pronounce it as 'ㅁ니다' or '씀니다'.
Check the last letter of the 어간 (stem)!
Remove '-다' and you get the stem.
Check the last vowel of the stem
Korean vowels are divided into two groups.
When the stem ends with a vowel, it merges with '았/었' and becomes shorter. This is called "contraction".
Words ending in '하다' always become 했습니다 / 했습니까.
When the last vowel of the stem is 'ㅡ', the 'ㅡ' disappears and you check the vowel of the syllable before it.
In some verbs ending with 받침 'ㄷ', the 'ㄷ' changes to 'ㄹ' before a vowel.
Not all ㄷ-ending verbs are irregular — these are regular; 'ㄷ' stays as-is and takes 았/었.
In some words ending with 받침 'ㅂ', the 'ㅂ' changes to '우' before a vowel.
Not all ㅂ-ending words are irregular — these are regular; 'ㅂ' stays as-is and takes 았/었.
When the stem ends with '르', it changes and an extra 'ㄹ' is added to the previous syllable.
In some verbs ending with 'ㅅ', the 'ㅅ' disappears before a vowel.
Check the last vowel, 받침, or shape of the stem and apply the matching rule.
Good news: The future tense is much easier than the past tense!
In the future tense, you only need to check the 받침 again. It works the same way as the present tense!
The future ending '~을 것입니다' starts with a vowel (ㅡ), so the irregulars that change before vowels reappear: ㄷ, ㅂ, ㅅ.
Note: 'ㅡ' and '르' irregulars have vowel-ending stems, so in the future tense they simply add 'ㄹ 것입니다' (regular). (e.g., 쓰다 → 쓸 것입니다, 다르다 → 다를 것입니다)
Check the last letter of the 어간 (stem)!
Check the last vowel, 받침, or shape of the stem and apply the matching rule.
Check the last letter of the 어간 (stem)!
No 받침 — add 'ㅂ' under the last letter, then 니다 (statement) or 니까 (question).
Has 받침 — just add 습니다 (statement) or 습니까 (question) right after the stem.
If 받침 is ㄹ — drop the ㄹ first, then follow Rule 🅐.
🅐 Last vowel ㅏ / ㅗ → 았습니다 / 았습니까
🅑 Last vowel not ㅏ / ㅗ → 었습니다 / 었습니까
🅐 No 받침 → ㄹ 것입니다 / ㄹ 것입니까
🅑 Has 받침 → 을 것입니다 / 을 것입니까
In English, the meaning is determined by the order of words.
"I" comes before the verb (eat) so it's the subject. "Rice" comes after the verb (eat) so it's the object.
In Korean, instead of word order, small letters attached after a word tell you its role.
Particles attach after a word and tell you "what role this word plays in the sentence."
Important: Many particles change their form depending on the 받침 of the word before them.
However, some particles always stay the same. (e.g., 에, 에서, 에게)
We will explain this one by one as you learn each particle.
You learned about 받침 when studying tenses. That same rule is used here too!
Let's look at an English sentence.
The word that does the action, like "I" here, is called "주어" (subject).
It's the same in Korean. Let's look at examples.
Like this, the answer to "who?" or "what?" is the subject.
In Korean, you attach "은/는" or "이/가" after the subject to say "this is the subject."
Both mean the same thing. Both mark the subject.
In general, "은/는" is used more often.
"이/가" is used when you want to emphasize the subject more.
But when you first learn Korean, you don't need to worry about this difference!
Whichever you use, both are the subject. Both are correct.
This subtle difference will become clear naturally as you learn more Korean.
For now, just remember this: Both 은/는 and 이/가 mark the subject!
The rule is simple. The form changes depending on the 받침 of the word before it.
Why are there two forms? To make pronunciation easier.
"빵는" is hard to say. "빵은" is easy!
*Special rule: "저 + 가" = "제가" ("저" changes to "제" for natural pronunciation.)
Let's look at an English sentence.
The word that receives the action, like "rice" here, is called "목적어" (object).
Simply put, the answer to "what?" is the object.
"을/를" is a particle that tells you this word is the object receiving the action.
In Korean, you attach "을" or "를" after the object to say "this is the object."
Summary So Far: Making a Sentence
"와/과" is used to connect two words. It is the same as "and" in English.
"나/이나" is used when choosing one from several options. It is the same as "or" in English.
"에게" is used when giving something or telling something to a person or animal.
It is the same as "to" in English.
"에게" always has the same form regardless of 받침!
"에" is attached before time words to tell you "when?"
It is the same as "at / in / on" in English.
Caution! Some time words do NOT use "에"!
These words already indicate "a time close to now" so they don't need "에."
"에서" is attached to a place word to tell you "where?"
It means "doing an action at/in a certain place."
It is the same as "at / in" in English.
"에서" also always has the same form regardless of 받침!
